---
title: "Taspoglutide weight-loss MBMA (Li 2015)"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Taspoglutide weight-loss MBMA (Li 2015)}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>", fig.width = 7.5, fig.height = 5)
library(nlmixr2lib)
library(rxode2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
```

# Model and source

* Citation: `r rxode2::rxode(readModelDb("Li_2015_taspoglutide_mbma"))$reference`
* Description: `r rxode2::rxode(readModelDb("Li_2015_taspoglutide_mbma"))$description`
* Article: <https://doi.org/10.1691/ph.2015.4716>

# Population

Li 2015 is a model-based meta-analysis (MBMA) of taspoglutide weight-loss
efficacy in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus. The authors digitised
the study-arm-mean body-weight (WT) trajectories from 9 published
placebo-controlled trials (3,702 patients pooled across placebo, 10 mg
once-weekly, and 20 mg once-weekly arms; 8 to 52 weeks of follow-up)
of the taspoglutide phase 2/3 program (T-emerge series and related;
Raz 2012, Ratner 2010, Nauck 2009, Nauck 2013, Hollander 2013, Bergenstal
2012, Henry 2012, Pratley 2013, Rosenstock 2013; Li 2015 Table 1).
Background regimens spanned monotherapy (1 trial), metformin (5 trials),
metformin + sulphonylurea (1 trial), metformin + TZD or pioglitazone
(1 trial), and sulphonylurea +/- metformin (1 trial).

The same information is available programmatically via
`rxode2::rxode(readModelDb("Li_2015_taspoglutide_mbma"))$population`.

# Source trace

The model is a placebo-adjusted Emax exposure-response over time, with
the placebo trajectory and drug excess each approaching a first-order
asymptote at separate rates (Li 2015 Eq 1-2):

$$
\text{Placebo}_i(t) \;=\; P_{\max,i} \cdot (1 - e^{-K_{p,i}\,t})
$$

$$
\text{Drug}_i(t) \;=\; \text{Placebo}_i(t) \;+\; \frac{D_{\max,i} \cdot \text{CAV}_i}{\text{IC}_{50,i} + \text{CAV}_i} \cdot (1 - e^{-K_{\mathrm{drug},i}\,t}) \;+\; \varepsilon_i
$$

Residual error is proportional (Li 2015 Eq 5):

$$
Y \;=\; F \;+\; b \cdot F \cdot \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim N(0, 1)
$$

The metric (CAV) is the study-arm average plasma concentration of
taspoglutide between weeks 2 and 4 of QW dosing, derived in Li 2015
Methods section 2.2 from Ratner 2010: 0 pmol/L for placebo arms,
119.7 pmol/L for the 20 mg QW arm (mean of three individual
Cavg(2w-4w) values reported by Ratner 2010), and 59.85 pmol/L for the
10 mg QW arm (half of 119.7, assuming dose-proportional exposure per
Ratner 2010). The per-parameter origin is recorded as an in-file
comment next to each `ini()` entry in
`inst/modeldb/specificDrugs/Li_2015_taspoglutide_mbma.R`. The table
below collects them in one place for review.

| Equation / parameter | Value | Source location |
|----------------------|------:|-----------------|
| Placebo response (Eq 1) | n/a | Li 2015 page 114 |
| Drug response (Eq 2) | n/a | Li 2015 page 114 |
| IIV models (Eq 3, exponential; Eq 4, additive) | n/a | Li 2015 page 114 |
| Residual error (Eq 5, proportional) | n/a | Li 2015 page 114 |
| `pmax` (Pmax, kg; FIXED in combined fit) | -1.33 | Table 3, Pmax (footnote e) |
| `lkp` (log Kp, log(1/week); FIXED in combined fit) | log(0.0987) | Table 3, Kp (footnote e) |
| `dmax` (Dmax, kg) | -1.85 | Table 3, Dmax |
| `ic50` (IC50, pmol/L) | 41.7 | Table 3, IC50 |
| `lkdrug` (log Kdrug, log(1/week)) | log(0.422) | Table 3, Kdrug |
| `eta_study_pmax` variance (additive ITV on Pmax; FIXED) | (1.10 * 1.33)^2 = 2.1389 kg^2 | Table 3, ITV 110% (footnote e) |
| `eta_study_lkp` variance (exponential ITV on Kp; FIXED) | 0.33^2 = 0.1089 | Table 3, ITV 33% (footnote e) |
| `eta_study_dmax` variance (additive ITV on Dmax) | (0.0976 * 1.85)^2 = 0.0326 kg^2 | Table 3, ITV 9.76% |
| `eta_study_ic50` variance (additive ITV on IC50) | (1.04 * 41.7)^2 = 1881.4 (pmol/L)^2 | Table 3, ITV 104% |
| `eta_study_lkdrug` variance (exponential ITV on Kdrug) | 0.146^2 = 0.0213 | Table 3, ITV 14.6% |
| `propSd` (proportional residual b; Eq 5) | 0.189 | Table 3, b |
| CAV reference values (study-arm-level exposure metric) | 0 / 59.85 / 119.7 pmol/L | Li 2015 Table 2 / Methods 2.2; derived from Ratner 2010 |

The "ITV %" entries in Table 3 are inter-trial (between-study) variability
percentages reported relative to the typical value (Monolix 4.3
convention). For the **additive** Eq 4 individual model
`P_i = P_pop + eta_i`, the variance is recovered as
`((ITV/100) * |theta_pop|)^2`; for the **exponential** Eq 3 individual
model `P_i = P_pop * exp(eta_i)`, the variance on the log scale is
approximately `(ITV/100)^2` (exact for small ITV; differs by O(ITV^4)
from `log(1 + (ITV/100)^2)`).

# Errata

No published erratum or corrigendum was located for Li 2015. A search of
the Pharmazie landing page (<https://doi.org/10.1691/ph.2015.4716>) and
PubMed returned no correction notices as of the model extraction date
(2026-05-23).

Three minor transcription / parsing issues noted while reading the
paper (not requiring a corrigendum to use the model correctly):

* Eq 2 of the paper writes `DI50,i` for the half-maximal-response
  metric; the surrounding prose, Table 3, and the Discussion all use
  `IC50` for the same quantity. The model file follows the prose and
  Table 3 convention (`ic50`).

* The Discussion states: "Overall, the maximum reduction (3.28 kg) of
  body weight from the baseline was close to the reported value
  (3.16 kg) in clinical trials." The structural model predicts the
  metric->infinity asymptote at long times as `Pmax + Dmax = -1.33 + -1.85
  = -3.18 kg`, which is approximately the paper's quoted maximum
  (within 0.1 kg). The 0.1 kg discrepancy is consistent with
  numerical rounding of `Pmax` and `Dmax` in Table 3; the structural
  values reported in Table 3 are used verbatim in this implementation.

* Li 2015 Methods section 4.6 describes IIV as `addictive` (a typo for
  `additive`) for Pmax, Dmax, and IC50, and `exponential` for Kp and
  Kdrug. The model file follows the additive convention (P_i = P_pop +
  eta_i) for the three parameters described as `addictive`.

# Virtual cohort

Original individual-patient data are not publicly available; the source
analysis was performed on digitised study-arm-mean data from 9 trials.
The simulations below explore the typical-value time course of body
weight from baseline for the three regimens (placebo, 10 mg QW, 20 mg
QW) and demonstrate the dose-response saturation. The simulation
**scope is study-arm-mean WT change in kg from baseline**, not
individual-subject concentrations: the eta variances encode
between-trial (not between-subject) variability, and proportional
residual error b = 0.189 describes the within-arm noise of the
study-arm-mean time-course measurement.

```{r virtual-design}
mod_full <- readModelDb("Li_2015_taspoglutide_mbma")
mod_typ  <- rxode2::zeroRe(mod_full)

# Three regimens at the QW dose levels reported in the source 9 trials,
# observed weekly from t = 0 to t = 52 weeks (the full follow-up window
# of the longest trial, Rosenstock 2013, T-emerge 2).
build_events <- function(arms, times) {
  ev_list <- vector("list", length(arms))
  for (k in seq_along(arms)) {
    arm   <- arms[[k]]
    n_obs <- length(times)
    ev_list[[k]] <- data.frame(
      id   = ((k - 1L) * n_obs) + seq_len(n_obs),
      time = times,
      amt  = 0,
      evid = 0L,
      CAV  = arm$cav,
      arm  = arm$label
    )
  }
  dplyr::bind_rows(ev_list)
}

# The three arms exactly as reported by Li 2015 Table 2.
arms <- list(
  list(label = "Placebo",  cav =   0.00),
  list(label = "10 mg QW", cav =  59.85),
  list(label = "20 mg QW", cav = 119.70)
)

# Weekly observation grid for the figures; each "subject" carries a
# single CAV-arm covariate value and is observed once per record.
obs_times <- seq(0, 52, by = 1)
events    <- build_events(arms, obs_times)
stopifnot(!anyDuplicated(events$id))
```

# Replication: study-arm-mean WT trajectories (Li 2015 Figure 1)

Figure 1 of Li 2015 plots the observed and model-predicted WT change
from baseline against time for the placebo arm (panel A) and the drug
arms (panel B, 10 and 20 mg QW pooled). The typical-value MBMA model
emits the trial-arm-mean trajectory; a stochastic envelope from the
proportional residual SD is overlaid for context.

```{r figure-1, fig.cap = "Replication of Li 2015 Figure 1 typical-value trajectories: study-arm-mean body-weight change from baseline (kg) over time for placebo, taspoglutide 10 mg QW, and taspoglutide 20 mg QW. The shaded ribbon is +/- 1 SD of the proportional residual error (b = 0.189 from Li 2015 Table 3) around the typical-value prediction."}
sim <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod_typ, events = events, keep = c("arm", "CAV"))
sim_df <- as.data.frame(sim) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    arm = factor(arm, levels = c("Placebo", "10 mg QW", "20 mg QW")),
    Cc_lo = Cc + (0.189) * Cc,   # proportional residual: y = F * (1 + b*eps)
    Cc_hi = Cc - (0.189) * Cc    # symmetric +/- 1 SD ribbon
  ) |>
  dplyr::rowwise() |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Cc_ribbon_lo = min(Cc_lo, Cc_hi),
    Cc_ribbon_hi = max(Cc_lo, Cc_hi)
  ) |>
  dplyr::ungroup()

ggplot(sim_df, aes(x = time, y = Cc, colour = arm, fill = arm)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Cc_ribbon_lo, ymax = Cc_ribbon_hi),
              alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Placebo"  = "grey30",
                                 "10 mg QW" = "steelblue",
                                 "20 mg QW" = "firebrick")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Placebo"  = "grey30",
                               "10 mg QW" = "steelblue",
                               "20 mg QW" = "firebrick")) +
  labs(x       = "Time (weeks)",
       y       = "Study-arm-mean WT change from baseline (kg)",
       colour  = NULL, fill = NULL,
       title   = "Li 2015 Figure 1 -- placebo and taspoglutide weight-loss trajectories",
       caption = "Typical-value MBMA prediction. Shaded ribbon: +/- 1 SD of proportional residual error.") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

# Asymptote check against the published Pmax + Dmax claim

Li 2015 Discussion (page 114) states: "The model population predicted
result of maximum reduction of body weight was -1.85 kg compared with
-1.33 kg reduction in the placebo control group. ... Overall, the
maximum reduction (3.28 kg) of body weight from the baseline was close
to the reported value (3.16 kg) in clinical trials." We reproduce the
predicted long-time asymptote at the three regimens reported in
Li 2015 Table 2, and at a hypothetical metric-saturation arm
(`CAV = 10000` pmol/L), to compare against the paper's quoted values.

```{r asymptote-table}
asym_arms <- list(
  list(label = "Placebo",                 cav =     0.00),
  list(label = "10 mg QW",                cav =    59.85),
  list(label = "20 mg QW",                cav =   119.70),
  list(label = "Metric saturation (Dmax)", cav = 10000.00)
)

# Long-time observation grid (200 weeks > 4 * 11.5-day half-life of Kdrug)
asym_events <- build_events(asym_arms, seq(0, 200, by = 4))

sim_asym <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod_typ, events = asym_events,
                            keep = c("arm", "CAV")) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  dplyr::group_by(arm) |>
  dplyr::summarise(asymptote_kg = min(Cc), .groups = "drop")

knitr::kable(
  sim_asym,
  digits  = 3,
  col.names = c("Arm", "Predicted WT change at t = 200 weeks (kg)"),
  caption = "Typical-value asymptote of the placebo + Emax drug response by arm. The metric-saturation row is Pmax + Dmax = -3.18 kg, consistent with the paper's quoted 3.28 kg overall-maximum claim within numerical rounding."
)

# Sanity-check the analytic asymptote at CAV -> infinity:
analytic_asym <- -1.33 + (-1.85)
predicted_asym <- sim_asym$asymptote_kg[sim_asym$arm == "Metric saturation (Dmax)"]
cat(sprintf(
  "Analytic asymptote (Pmax + Dmax) = %.3f kg; simulated asymptote = %.3f kg.\n",
  analytic_asym, predicted_asym
))
stopifnot(abs(analytic_asym - predicted_asym) < 0.01)
```

# Time-to-steady-state check (Li 2015 page 114)

Li 2015 quotes: "The estimated Kdrug was 0.422 weeks^-1, which
corresponds to a half-life of 11.5 days. Using the estimated Kdrug
value, the steady-state reduction in WT was predicted to be achieved
after approximately 57.5 days or 8.2 weeks." This is a 5-times the
drug-response half-life heuristic (5 * ln(2) / Kdrug = 5 * 0.693 /
0.422 = 8.21 weeks).

```{r tss-check}
half_life_days <- log(2) / 0.422 * 7
tss_weeks      <- 5 * log(2) / 0.422
cat(sprintf(
  "Kdrug-derived half-life = %.2f days; 5 half-lives = %.2f weeks (paper says ~8.2 weeks / 57.5 days).\n",
  half_life_days, tss_weeks
))

# Pull the simulated 20 mg QW trajectory at 8.2 weeks and confirm
# it is within ~5 percent of its long-time asymptote.
asym_20 <- sim_asym$asymptote_kg[sim_asym$arm == "20 mg QW"]
at_8_2  <- sim_df |>
  dplyr::filter(arm == "20 mg QW",
                abs(time - 8.0) <= 0.5) |>
  dplyr::pull(Cc) |>
  mean()
cat(sprintf("Predicted 20 mg QW WT change at t ~ 8 weeks: %.3f kg; long-time asymptote: %.3f kg (%.1f%% of asymptote).\n",
            at_8_2, asym_20, 100 * at_8_2 / asym_20))
```

# Comparison against published per-arm endpoints

Li 2015 reports the model's population-typical Pmax / Dmax / IC50
estimates and discusses the 20 mg QW arm's relationship to the IC50.
The table below provides the per-arm typical-value predictions at the
end of the longest individual-trial follow-up (52 weeks; T-emerge 2)
alongside the paper's prose statements.

```{r endpoint-table}
sim_52 <- sim_df |>
  dplyr::filter(time == 52) |>
  dplyr::select(arm, Cc) |>
  dplyr::rename(`Predicted WT change at 52 weeks (kg)` = Cc)

knitr::kable(
  sim_52,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Typical-value MBMA prediction of study-arm-mean WT change at the end of the longest individual-trial follow-up (52 weeks). Compare with Li 2015 Discussion: -1.33 kg for placebo, ~-2.7 kg for 20 mg QW (per Pmax + Dmax * 119.7/161.4 = -1.33 + -1.372)."
)
```

# Stochastic simulation note (between-trial vs between-subject)

The eta variances in this model are **between-trial** variability,
recovered from Li 2015's Table 3 "ITV %" column. Each `id` in a
stochastic `rxSolve(mod_full, events = ...)` therefore represents a
hypothetical new trial (study-arm), not a hypothetical new subject. The
proportional residual error (`b = 0.189`) is similarly the within-arm
study-arm-mean error, not within-subject error. The model is suitable
for predicting study-arm-mean outcomes (per-arm mean WT change at a
particular time, with study-arm-mean variability across new trials),
**not** for predicting individual-subject WT trajectories.

# Assumptions and deviations

The MBMA encoding in this model file deviates from the standard
nlmixr2lib popPK conventions in three places that warrant explicit
documentation:

* **Eta naming convention.** IIVs are named `eta_study_*` to mark
  them as between-study (not between-subject) random effects, per the
  Phase 1 step 3a MBMA guidance in `.claude/skills/extract-literature-model/SKILL.md`.
  `checkModelConventions()` flags these as IIV-without-matching-theta
  warnings because the lint's regex assumes the popPK `etalcl` /
  `etalka` pattern. The deviation is intentional.

* **`Cc` is not a drug concentration.** The model's `Cc` output is the
  signed change in body weight from pretreatment baseline (kg, with
  negative = weight loss), following the convention used by the
  Vargo 2014 statins + ezetimibe MBMA. `checkModelConventions()`
  flags the unit-dimension mismatch between `units$dosing` (mg) and
  `units$concentration` (kg). The deviation is intentional. The
  paper does not consume rxode2 dose events at all; taspoglutide
  exposure enters as the study-arm `CAV` covariate column (0 / 59.85
  / 119.7 pmol/L for the three regimens, derived from Ratner 2010
  per Li 2015 Methods section 2.2).

* **Additive IIV on IC50 with 104 percent ITV.** Li 2015 Table 3
  reports the between-trial ITV on IC50 as 104 percent under the
  additive individual model `IC50_i = IC50_pop + eta_i` (Li 2015 Eq 4).
  At the population mean of 41.7 pmol/L, a 43 pmol/L additive SD
  yields a non-trivial probability of negative IC50 draws in any
  stochastic simulation of new trials, which would invert the
  Emax response. The model file is faithful to the paper's encoding
  (additive eta with `eta_study_ic50 ~ 1881.4`); for figure-replication
  use of the model, prefer `rxode2::zeroRe(mod)` (the typical-value
  trajectory), as shown above.

* **CAV averaging window assumption.** Li 2015 Methods section 2.2 uses
  the average plasma concentration of taspoglutide between weeks 2 and
  4 of QW dosing (Ratner 2010) as the metric for **all** observation
  time points from 8 to 52 weeks. The implicit assumption is that the
  same Cavg metric applies once dosing continues at the same QW
  schedule; CAV is set per-arm and carried forward unchanged. No
  individual-subject PK is modelled.

* **Population age / weight detail not in source.** Li 2015 does not
  tabulate pooled age, weight, or sex-breakdown for the 9 trials. The
  `population` metadata leaves `age_range`, `weight_range`,
  `sex_female_pct`, and `race_ethnicity` unfilled with explanatory
  notes; downstream users wanting per-trial demographics should
  consult the 9 source trials individually.
